---
title: "Follow-up workshop: {{< var md-02.title >}}"
editor: source
---

::: {.callout-note}
## This is an offer, not yet a date

This page sketches the follow-up workshop to [Git for Scientists](index.qmd).
Dates are not set. If you want to take part, email
[{{< var email >}}](mailto:{{< var email >}}) and you will hear from me as
soon as a date is fixed.
:::

## {{< fa circle-info >}} Background

In the workshop you learned the collaboration loop: branch, pull request,
review, merge. This follow-up adds the layer above it: how a research team
decides *what* to work on, keeps that work visible, and tracks it toward a
deadline, using three GitHub tools that live right next to your code:

- **Issues**: one task, written down with enough context that someone else
  could pick it up
- **Project boards**: all open tasks of a team on one board, moving from
  "Todo" to "Done"
- **Milestones**: issues grouped toward a deadline, such as a manuscript
  submission or a grant deliverable

The same skills are now the entry point for working with AI coding agents.
On GitHub, an issue can be assigned to an agent such as Claude, which then
does the work and opens a pull request for your review, and project boards
show the live status of agent sessions alongside human work. A well-written
issue has quietly become the highest-leverage AI skill for a research team:
a vague issue now produces a bad pull request, not just confusion in a
teammate.

## {{< fa bullseye >}} Learning objectives

By the end of this workshop, participants will be able to:

1. Write an issue that a colleague, or an AI coding agent, can act on
   without asking follow-up questions: background, goal, and acceptance
   criteria.

2. Set up a project board for a research project, add issues and pull
   requests to it, and keep its status columns honest during daily work.

3. Group issues into a milestone with a due date, read the progress bar,
   and decide when a milestone (deadline-shaped) or a board
   (workflow-shaped) is the right tool.

4. Hand a well-scoped issue to an AI coding agent and review the resulting
   pull request with the same review skills used for a colleague's work.

## {{< fa clock >}} Format

A half-day session (about 3 hours including a break), in the same rhythm as
the first workshop: short slides, my turn (watch), your turn (do). Every
exercise runs on the repositories you created in Git for Scientists.

| Block | Working title | You will |
|-------|---------------|----------|
| P1 | From to-do list to issues | Turn a shared research to-do list into well-written issues on your manuscript repository |
| P2 | The project board | Build a team board, add your issues, and work the board while you work the tasks |
| P3 | Milestones | Create a "Submission" milestone, attach issues, and watch the progress bar do its job |
| P4 | Hand-off to an agent | Watch a well-scoped issue be assigned to Claude Code, then review the pull request it opens |

## {{< fa graduation-cap >}} Prerequisites

Participation in Git for Scientists, or equivalent: you can clone a
repository, commit and push from RStudio, open a pull request, and review
one.

## {{< fa book >}} Materials this builds on

The teaching material adapts openly licensed lessons from the scientific
computing community:

1. LTER / NCEAS Scientific Computing workshop
   ["Collaborative Coding with GitHub"](https://lter.github.io/workshop-github/),
   in particular the [milestones lesson](https://lter.github.io/workshop-github/milestones.html)
   and the project boards chapter
2. NCEAS Learning Hub module
   [Using GitHub for Project Management](https://nceas.github.io/learning-hub-modules/git-and-github/github-for-project-mgmt/)
3. Openscapes Champions lesson
   [GitHub for project management](https://openscapes.github.io/series/core-lessons/github/github-issues.html)
